data.title: CHAPTER 6 — ITERATION AND REVISION LOOPS
blocks: After this chapter you will be able to:
Chapter 6 — Iteration and Revision Loops — Diagnose what failed in an output before sending a revision prompt Name the specific change target so Claude knows what

S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — THE CORE PROBLEM: DRIFT WITHOU

data.title: THE CORE PROBLEM: DRIFT WITHOUT DIAGNOSIS
blocks: · Diagnose. Name what the output did wrong and why.
· Target. Specify which element should change.
· Preserve. Name what must not change.
· Verify. Ask for a change log or comparison so you can confirm what moved.

S03.meta: // SLIDE 03 — BEFORE / AFTER: A REVISION PRO

data.title: BEFORE / AFTER: A REVISION PROMPT WALKTHROUGH
blocks: Names the failure categories (tone, structure) separately
Constrains the edit (facts unchanged, no new claims)
Requests a change log for audit

data.title: THE DIAGNOSE-REVISE-COMPARE PATTERN.
blocks: When you are uncertain which revision direction is better, use compare-and-select. Ask Claude to produce two versions of
The Diagnose-Revise-Compare Pattern — The transition between section 2 and section 3 is abrupt. The reader needs to understand that we are moving from the pro

data.title: THE CHANGE LOG REQUEST.
blocks: · What was moved (and where it went)
· What language was changed (and what principle guided the change)
· What was kept exactly as written

data.title: PRESERVE CONSTRAINTS EXPLICITLY.
blocks: · Keep all statistics exactly as written
· Keep the three-part structure (problem / approach / outcome)
· Do not add new examples or claims
· Do not change the title

S08.meta: // SLIDE 08 — WHEN TO START OVER, NOT REVISE

data.title: WHEN TO START OVER, NOT REVISE.
blocks: · Wrong task entirely. Claude misunderstood the goal. Every revision will be trying to fix an output built on a wrong foun
· Wrong structure. The argument is organized around the wrong framework. Line-by-line revision will not fix a structural p
· Compounding errors. Each revision introduces a new problem as it fixes the old one. The output is deteriorating, not imp
· Constraint violations accumulate. If you have revised three times and each version violates a different constraint, the
data.section: WHEN TO START OVER, NOT REVISE

data.title: SELF-CRITIQUE PROMPTS
blocks: · Does it address the audience I described?
· Does every claim rest on the sources I provided?
· Is the structure appropriate for a reader who will skim first and

data.title: REUSABLE REVISION TEMPLATE.
blocks: What you changed
What principle guided the change
What you kept exactly as written

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Sending "make it better" without a diagnosis.
Revising everything at once.
Skipping the change log on long documents.
Iterating past the point of diminishing returns.
Using self-critique as fact-checking.

data.title: THE CORE PROBLEM: DRIFT WITHOUT DIAGNOSI
blocks: A vague revision prompt tells Claude to change something but not what or why. The model fills the gap with inference — i
Revision drift is not a model failure. It is a specification failure. The work order was incomplete. The fix is to treat
